intro:
The bridegroom is one of Scripture's most intimate images for God's relationship to his people. In the biblical world a bridegroom was the man who, having paid the bride-price and made his vows, came to take his bride to himself in covenant love; the wedding was a joy-filled celebration sealing a binding union. Scripture lifts this picture out of the village wedding and makes it a window into the love of God — the LORD is the husband who weds himself to a people, and the covenant is, at heart, a marriage.

The image runs from the Law and Prophets onward. At Sinai the LORD bound Israel to himself in a covenant the prophets later read as a marriage, so that idolatry became adultery and apostasy a broken wedding vow. "Your Maker is your husband," Isaiah declares (Isaiah 54:5), and "as the bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so shall your God rejoice over you" (Isaiah 62:5). Hosea dramatizes the LORD's faithful love for an unfaithful bride and his promise to betroth her again forever in righteousness and steadfast love (Hosea 2:19–20); Jeremiah and Ezekiel grieve Israel's harlotry against her husband (Jeremiah 2:2; Ezekiel 16:8). The Song of Songs gives the love of bride and bridegroom its fullest poetic voice.

The shadow finds its substance in Christ. John the Baptist names Jesus "the bridegroom" and calls himself only "the friend of the bridegroom" who rejoices to hear his voice (John 3:29). Jesus claims the title for himself: the wedding guests cannot fast "while the bridegroom is with them," but a day comes when the bridegroom is taken away (Mark 2:19–20), and his parable of the ten virgins turns on readiness for the bridegroom's coming (Matthew 25:1–13). Paul unfolds the mystery: a husband's love for his wife images "Christ and the church" — Christ "loved the church and gave himself up for her" to present her to himself in splendor, holy and without blemish (Ephesians 5:25–32).

So the whole story moves toward a wedding. The God who courted an unfaithful people now wins a pure bride at the cost of his own blood, and history closes not with a battle only but with "the marriage of the Lamb," his bride made ready (Revelation 19:7), the new Jerusalem coming down "as a bride adorned for her husband" (Revelation 21:2). To call Christ the bridegroom is to say that salvation is not merely rescue but union — to be loved, claimed, and joined to him forever.
